title: Dog Swallowed a Plastic Toy: Triage Guide and Emergency Steps
meta_desc: If your dog swallowed a plastic toy, it is a high-urgency situation. Learn the risks of obstruction and when to seek emergency veterinary care.
content_html:
What is a Foreign Body Ingestion?

When a dog swallows a plastic toy, it is medically referred to as a foreign body ingestion. Because plastic is indigestible, it can become lodged in the esophagus, stomach, or intestines, potentially leading to a life-threatening gastrointestinal obstruction or internal damage from sharp edges.

Is This an Emergency?

The urgency level for this situation is High. You should contact a veterinarian or an emergency animal hospital immediately. Plastic objects pose a significant risk of causing a physical blockage that prevents food and water from passing through the digestive system. If your dog is experiencing vomiting, abdominal pain, loss of appetite, or lethargy, they require immediate medical intervention. Do not attempt to induce vomiting at home, as the toy could cause further damage or become a choking hazard on the way back up.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

If you have an identical toy or the pieces left behind from the chewed object, take a photo to show the veterinarian. Having a visual reference of the size, shape, and sharpness of the plastic helps the medical team determine the best course of action, such as whether the object can be retrieved via endoscopy or if surgical intervention is necessary.

faqs.question: How long does it take for a dog to pass a plastic toy?
faqs.answer: Objects can take anywhere from 10 to 24 hours to move through the digestive tract, but many plastic toys are too large or rigid to pass safely. You should never wait for it to pass without veterinary supervision.

faqs.question: What are the signs of a bowel obstruction?
faqs.answer: Common signs include repeated vomiting, bloating, straining to defecate, dehydration, and a painful abdomen when touched.

faqs.question: Should I feed my dog to help the toy pass?
faqs.answer: No, do not feed your dog until you have consulted a vet. Adding food to the stomach can worsen an obstruction or complicate the administration of anesthesia if surgery is required.
